title: Register and Corpus-Informed Usage
description: How English choices shift across conversation, academic prose, public information, and professional communication.
upgradePremise: Expanded for Nyx and Marion with stronger grammatical reasoning, case-based analysis, modules, projects, and explicit source coverage.

forNyx: Prefer precise, grammatical phrasing that sounds natural rather than stiff.
Use register-sensitive wording so conversational output and formal output do not collapse into one style.
When uncertain, preserve meaning first, then improve form through revision rather than over-correcting.
forMarion: Use the pack to explain why a wording choice works, not just whether it is 'right' or 'wrong'.
Ground revision advice in form-function reasoning, register, audience, and evidence.
Support coaching, diagnostics, and portfolio-style improvement tracking.

topics.name: Register Differences (High-Level)
coreClaims: Academic prose tends to compress information (nominalizations, dense noun phrases).
Conversation tends to be interactive (pronouns, stance markers, ellipsis, discourse particles).
practice: Convert conversational phrasing into academic register while preserving meaning.
Spot register-inappropriate forms (not errors, but mismatches).

topics.name: Frequency-Driven Reasoning
coreClaims: When multiple grammatical options exist, distribution by register helps decide which to use.
A usage decision can be supported by: (a) register expectations, (b) clarity goals, (c) audience norms.
practice: Write a brief 'usage justification' note for one contested form (e.g., passive voice in methods sections).

topics.name: Usage Diagnostic Workflow
coreClaims: High-performing students treat usage as a debugging task: identify pattern → consult reference → test in context.
Error logs work best when grouped by pattern (articles, prepositions, complement frames).
practice: Maintain a 20-item error log with: pattern, example, correction, rule-of-thumb, register note.

modules.name: Register Contrast
outcomes: Differentiate conversation, news, academic, and public-service language.
Recognize mismatches that sound odd without being strictly ungrammatical.

modules.name: Frequency and Choice
outcomes: Use corpus-style reasoning to compare alternatives.
Support usage choices with audience and genre logic.

modules.name: Debugging Usage
outcomes: Pair each pattern with a reference note and example set.

caseStudies.title: Marion Answers Like a Journal Article to a Casual User
caseStudies.scenario: The content is correct but the register is too compressed and impersonal for a conversational setting.
intervention: Preserve precision while easing processing load.

caseStudies.title: Nyx Becomes Too Casual in Formal Context
caseStudies.scenario: Friendly phrasing starts to sound under-qualified in grant, legal, or academic contexts.
intervention: Use field-appropriate reporting verbs and paragraph frames.

projects.name: Register Switchboard
projects.goal: Build prompts and examples that convert one meaning across conversational, academic, public-service, and professional styles.

referenceTextsUsed.title: Longman Grammar of Spoken and Written English
referenceTextsUsed.citationAPA: Biber, D., Johansson, S., Leech, G., Conrad, S., & Finegan, E. (1999). Longman grammar of spoken and written English. Longman.

referenceTextsUsed.title: Practical English Usage
referenceTextsUsed.citationAPA: Swan, M. (2016). Practical English usage (4th ed.). Oxford University Press.

normalizationMeta.lineAuditPass: full-file structural and runtime retrieval audit

keywords: a usage decision can be supported by

auditSummary.notes: Normalized for consistent Marion retrieval, alias routing, and source coverage tracking.

definitionTerms.definition: Register is the way language choices shift according to context, audience, purpose, medium, and relationship.
A corpus is a structured collection of real language examples used to study patterns of usage.
Usage is the pattern of how words, structures, and expressions are used by speakers and writers in context.
Plain language is communication designed so the intended audience can find, understand, and use the information easily.
A register mismatch happens when language is grammatically possible but unsuitable for the audience, genre, or situation.

runtimeDefinitionIndex.fallbackRule: If term appears here, answer directly. Do not ask for more detail unless the user requests a specific register, example, or comparison.

rules: If the user asks 'what is', 'define', 'meaning of', or 'explain' plus a known English term, answer directly from this domain before generic fallback.
Do not route English definition questions into technical_debug unless the user explicitly asks for backend, code, file, audit, autopsy, patch, route, script, endpoint, deployment, or validation.
If a term overlaps with another domain, give a brief neutral definition, name the possible English interpretation, and ask one targeted clarification question.
Preserve meaning first; revise form second; never over-correct when the user asks for explanation rather than rewriting.
No user-facing generic fallback such as 'I need one more concrete detail' when the term is present in this pack. Use answer + clarification instead.
blockedFallbacks: I need one more concrete detail to route this cleanly.
I’ll keep this broad and practical
